1:
The chapter opens on a sentence the witnesses treat as a theology of providence in miniature: the war between the two houses is long, but its direction is settled. The grammatical-historical commentators define the nature of the conflict. Charles Ellicott insists it was "not actual fighting of pitched battles, but a state of hostility," in which Ishbosheth and David each claimed the whole nation's allegiance until Ishbosheth's death; the pretender was too weak to wage open war, and David "was content to abide the fulfilment of the promises of the Lord in His own good time." He points to the steady drift of strength recorded in 1 Chronicles 12:19-22, men coming over to David "day by day," so that as one house rose the other of necessity sank.

The reformed John Gill supplies the chronology the verse compresses: the great victory of the previous chapter did not end the war, which "lasted five years longer," waged now not in pitched battles but in skirmishes, until at Ishbosheth's death David, who had already reigned above seven years in Hebron, was made king over all Israel. John Wesley, of the wesleyan-arminian voices, agrees on the arithmetic — the five further years and the seven in Hebron — reading Ishbosheth as crowned soon after Saul's death.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the two lessons the passage yields. First, the length of the war is providential pedagogy: though truth and equity had David's side, and therefore God's, "God made for wise and holy ends prolonged the conflict," to try David's faith and patience and make his settlement the sweeter. Henry loves the analogy it offers the believer — the long war between grace and corruption, "the flesh lusting against the spirit, and the spirit against the flesh," in which corruption, like the house of Saul, "grows weaker and weaker," while grace, like the house of David, "grows stronger and stronger," till judgment be brought forth unto victory. Second, the increase of David's house — six sons by six wives in the seven Hebron years — is noted as strengthening his interest, yet Henry will not gloss over the fault: it was "contrary to the law" (Deuteronomy 17:17) and a bad example, and he bids us "rejoice with trembling in the building up of our families," since three of these sons — Amnon, Absalom, Adonijah — proved infamous. The witnesses do not divide; together they read the slow, sure decline of Saul's house as the pattern of how God brings his anointed to the throne, and every sanctified soul to victory, not by a single stroke but by a long and testing war.
2:
The narrative turns from the war to the nursery, and the witnesses agree that this list of sons is not a digression but the promised proof that "the house of David waxed stronger." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch make the point explicitly: the account of the sons born at Hebron "does not break the thread," but is "very appropriately introduced here, as a practical proof of the strengthening of the house of David," in keeping with the custom of opening a king's reign with notices of his family, as elsewhere in the books of Kings. A parallel list stands in 1 Chronicles 3:1-4, and the first two sons were born of the two wives David had brought with him to Hebron (1 Samuel 25:42-43).

The firstborn is Amnon, of Ahinoam the Jezreelitess. The reformed John Gill observes that David "was married before he came there, had wives in his state of exile, but had no children by them there, at least no sons," so that the fruitfulness now recorded belongs peculiarly to the Hebron reign and marks its blessing. Keil notes the small textual matter that the written form is "probably only a copyist's error" for the ordinary passive, "were born."

Beneath the bare genealogy the witnesses hear a sober undertone. This Amnon, named first with all a firstborn's promise, is the very son whose crime against Tamar will one day set the house of David tearing at itself; the reader who knows the sequel feels the shadow already lying across the birth-notice. The commentators are content here to record and to date — six sons in seven years, each a fresh stake in the commonwealth's safety, each child "whose welfare was embarked in the common safety" a security given to the throne. Yet the very multiplication that strengthens David politically is the same multiplication the law had warned against, and the roll of names is also, quietly, a roll of griefs to come. The witnesses do not debate the verse; they simply let it stand as the historian meant it — evidence of a rising house, and the first entry in a family record that will prove David a great king and a much-tried father.
3:
The roll continues with the second and third sons, and the witnesses fasten on two puzzles: the double name of the second son, and the ominous marriage that produced the third. On the second son the reformed John Gill notes that he is called Chileab here but Daniel in 1 Chronicles 3:1. Daniel, he judges, was the name David first gave — "God hath judged me," a memorial that God had pleaded his cause against Nabal, whose widow Abigail was the boy's mother (1 Samuel 25:39). Why "Chileab"? The name is commonly taken to mean "like unto his father" or "all father"; the child, Gill reports, "had all the features of his father, and was exceedingly like him." He mentions but declines to relay the rabbinic legend attached to the name.

The third son is Absalom, "the son of Maacah the daughter of Talmai king of Geshur." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply place the pedigree in its list, but the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads it with a moralist's eye. Geshur, he explains, was "a part of Syria, northward from the land of Israel," and David married its princess "as it may seem, in policy, that he might have a powerful friend and ally to assist him against Ishbosheth's party in the north." Then comes the sting: "he paid dear for making piety give place to policy, as the history of Absalom sheweth." The alliance that looked like statecraft bred the son who would one day drive his father from Jerusalem and die in the branches of an oak.

So the verse, brief as it is, carries the witnesses' shared conviction that the private history of David's marriages is inseparable from the public history of his kingdom. Chileab, who resembled his father and vanishes early from the record, is set beside Absalom, half-Aramean by his mother and half-rebel by his own will. Gill's care over a name and Wesley's warning over a wedding meet in a single point: David's household, gathered in these Hebron years as a token of strength, held within it both the son who mirrored him and the son who would nearly destroy him. The genealogy is thus already a lesson in the cost of counting policy dearer than piety.
4:
This verse names the fourth and fifth sons, and only one witness pauses over it — but the pause is telling. The reformed John Gill lets the list run, yet cannot pass Adonijah, "the son of Haggith," without noting who this fourth son would become: "the same that usurped the throne before his father's death, to anticipate Solomon, and died by his order" (1 Kings 1:5; 1 Kings 2:24-25). A single clause of genealogy thus opens onto the last great crisis of David's reign, when the old king lay dying and his fourth son "exalted himself, saying, I will be king."

The historian's method here rewards the reader who knows the sequel. In the bare formula — the fourth, Adonijah; the fifth, Shephatiah the son of Abital — the witnesses see the same providence at work that governs the whole chapter. These sons are the visible increase of a rising house, and yet the very list that proves David strong is seeded with the men who will trouble his peace. Amnon, Absalom, Adonijah: three of the six named so far will bring their father grief, and the fourth's rebellion will echo the third's. Where Absalom seized his moment in his father's prime, Adonijah will seize his at his father's death; both will fail, and both will die.

Gill's terse cross-reference is itself an act of synthesis, binding the beginning of David's family to its end. The verse teaches, by its very economy, that greatness and grief grow from the same root. A quiver full of children is a blessing the psalmist celebrates (Psalms 127:5), but the record of David's quiver is a record of arrows that turned in the hand. The witnesses do not moralize at length here; the facts do the work. To name Adonijah is to remember that a crown contested from without, as it was through the long war of this chapter, may be contested again from within, by a son of the king's own house. The house of David waxes stronger — and the seeds of its future sorrows are numbered among its strengths.
5:
The list of Hebron sons closes with Ithream, "by Eglah David's wife," and the witnesses gather around the single odd phrase: of all six mothers, only this last is expressly called "David's wife." The reformed John Gill lays out the readings. Some suppose Eglah "a person of no note, and had nothing else to distinguish her," so that she is marked only by her relation to David — though Gill objects that the same could be said of the two preceding mothers. Others take her to be "his beloved wife, his heifer, as her name signifies," Eglah meaning a heifer; and hence some of the Jews identify her with Michal, David's first and best-loved wife.

Gill weighs that identification and rejects it on plain grounds. Michal "as yet was not returned to David in Hebron," and when she was restored she "did not seem to continue there long enough to have a son there"; besides, being his first wife she "would not be reckoned last." He is equally dismissive of a further rabbinic notion that Eglah was Saul's widow. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley canvasses the same two possibilities without deciding: either she was "of obscure parentage, and was known by no other title but her relation to David," or she was "his first and most proper wife, best known by her other name of Michal," who though childless after she scorned David's dancing (2 Samuel 6:23) "might have one before that time." Wesley adds that she may be "named the last, because she was given away from David, and married to another man."

Neither witness will force the puzzle to a solution, and their restraint is itself instructive. What both draw from the verse is not a settled identification but a settled arithmetic and a settled warning. Wesley states it flatly: "six sons in seven years," and observes that "some have had as numerous an offspring, and with much more honour and comfort, by one wife." The tally that the historian offers as proof of a strengthening house the commentators receive with sober qualification — "we know not that any of the six were famous: but three were very infamous." So the closing name of the list, wrapped in its small mystery, becomes the occasion for the same lesson the whole genealogy has been teaching: a full house is not the same as a happy one, and David's many wives, contrary to the law, purchased him political weight at the price of domestic peace.
6:
With the family list closed, the narrative returns to the war, and the verse serves as a hinge: the house of David rose, but "Abner made himself strong for the house of Saul." The witnesses agree that this clause is the key to everything that follows — the tottering dynasty stood on one man. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch tie the notice tightly to the story: while the war continued, "Abner adhered firmly to the house of Saul," yet in that very strength lay the seed of its ruin, for he had appropriated one of Saul's concubines and would turn on Ishbosheth the moment he was charged with it. Keil judges that Abner "had certainly perceived the utter incapacity of Ishbosheth for a very long time, if not from the very outset, and had probably made him king" himself. Charles Ellicott makes the point plainly: "the fortunes of the house of Saul depended entirely upon Abner," and the verse is set here precisely to explain the story of the quarrel that follows.

The reformed John Gill reads the clause temporally — Abner's strengthening of Saul's house "continued as long as that war did," until the following quarrel broke it. The wesleyan-arminian voices sharpen the motive. John Wesley notes that Abner "used all his endeavours to support Saul's house," a fact "mentioned to shew the reason of his deep resentment of the following aspersion." Adam Clarke goes further, reading Abner's self-strengthening as ambition already reaching for the crown: this "strengthening of himself, and going in to the late king's concubine, were most evident proofs that he wished to seize upon the government."

Here the witnesses touch a difference of emphasis rather than a real dispute. Clarke sees Abner grasping at the throne through Rizpah, since among the Hebrews the harem of a dead king passed to his successor and to take a royal concubine was to assert a royal claim (2 Samuel 16:21; 1 Kings 2:22). Ellicott doubts Abner had any such design, "since he was exerting himself to maintain Ishbosheth on the throne." Whether the act was calculated treason or careless presumption, all agree on its effect: it exposed how completely Saul's house leaned on its captain, so that when Abner "failed them, they would fall of course." The verse quietly announces that the pillar of the falling house is about to be knocked away — and by the very king it upheld.
7:
Now the pillar is struck. Ishbosheth charges Abner with taking Rizpah, Saul's concubine, and the witnesses read the accusation as the folly that lost the house of Saul its last defender. The grammatical-historical commentators explain why the charge stung so deeply. Charles Ellicott notes that Rizpah is the same woman whose "strong and tender grief over the loss of her sons" is recorded later (2 Samuel 21:8-11), and, more to the point, that "the harem of an Eastern monarch was considered as the property of his successor," so that to take a woman from it was "the assertion of a claim to the throne" (compare 2 Samuel 12:8; 1 Kings 2:22). It is this implied charge of treason, not mere immorality, "that so greatly rouses the anger of Abner." Keil and Delitzsch gather the verse and the next into one long period, observing that the whole reason Ishbosheth's concubine is mentioned is to be gathered from his reproachful question, and that Ishbosheth's very name has dropped from the Hebrew text but is rightly restored from the versions.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the political stupidity of the accusation. Whether the thing was true or not, "it would have been Ishbosheth's prudence to be silent, considering how much it was his interest not to disoblige Abner." If the charge was false, it was "very disingenuous and ungrateful to entertain unjust surmises of one who had ventured his all for him"; and Henry, weighing Abner's evasive reply, suspects the deed was in fact done, "for he does not expressly deny it." Yet he draws the larger providence: "God can serve his own purposes by the sins and follies of men," and Ishbosheth's rash word, springing from no wisdom of his own, becomes the hinge on which the kingdom turns to David.

The reformed John Gill stands with the same reading of Abner's mind: if the charge was false Abner "had a good deal of reason" for his wrath, and if true, "he thought he deserved better at his hands, than to be reproved for and upbraided with what he might think was a very small fault," especially by one he had so served. The witnesses do not divide over the facts they cannot settle — whether Abner was guilty — but they are one on the meaning: a weak king, mishandling the one man who held his throne, turned a private suspicion into a public catastrophe, and God overruled the folly to fulfil his promise to David.
8:
Abner's fury breaks out in a torrent of self-justification, and the witnesses concentrate on his opening retort — "Am I a dog's head?" — and on the indignation of a man who thinks his loyalty repaid with insult. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke paraphrases the outburst with force: "Dost thou treat a man with indignity who has been the only prop of thy tottering kingdom, and the only person who could make head against the house of David?" The dog's head is the very emblem of contempt, and Abner flings it back at the master who dared reprove him.

The grammatical-historical commentators refine the phrase itself. Charles Ellicott corrects the received rendering: the clause taken from the Vulgate, "Am I a dog's head?", is "hardly possible"; it should rather be "Am I a dog's head belonging to Judah?" — Abner protesting that he is being treated as though he were a contemptible traitor secretly holding with David's side. Keil and Delitzsch unfold the whole of his answer in the same key: "Am I a dog's head, holding with Judah? To-day I show affection to the house of Saul thy father, towards his brethren and his friends, and did not let thee fall into the hand of David, and thou reproachest me to-day with the fault with the woman?" The word for a "dog's head," Keil notes, "is something thoroughly contemptible"; Abner's grievance is that he has spent himself for Saul's house against Judah, the very tribe destined to prevail, and is rewarded with a charge over "this woman."

The reformed John Gill reads the psychology exactly. If the accusation was false, Abner "had a good deal of reason" to be wroth; if true, he plainly reckoned it a trifle, thinking "he deserved better at his hands, than to be reproved for and upbraided with what he might think was a very small fault," and that "in one that had been so serviceable to him." Here the witnesses expose the moral core of the scene: Abner never denies the deed. His answer is all wounded pride and calculated menace, not innocence. The commentators see in the "dog's head" the self-portrait of a proud man who cannot bear reproof from one he thinks he has obliged, and whose swollen sense of merit is about to make him a kingmaker for David out of sheer revenge.
9:
Abner's rage rises to an oath, and the witnesses fix on its startling content: he swears to do for David the very thing he had spent years opposing — to translate the kingdom from Saul's house to David's, "as the LORD hath sworn to him." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the vow and probe its ground: "God do so to Abner ... as Jehovah hath sworn to David, so will I do to him," to take the kingdom from Saul's house and set David's throne "over Israel and over Judah, from Dan to Beersheba." They concede that "we do not know of any oath with which God had promised the kingdom to David," but hold that "the promise of God in itself is equivalent to an oath," God being unable to lie (1 Samuel 15:29; Numbers 23:19), and that this promise "was generally known in Israel."

The other witnesses press the same difficulty and add a sharper indictment. Charles Ellicott agrees there is "no record of a Divine oath," but takes "the prophetic declaration that God's choice of him was unalterable" (1 Samuel 15:29) to carry an oath's force. He then exposes Abner's motive without mercy: Abner "does not propose to do this in order to fulfil the Divine will, for his words show that he had been acting hitherto in conscious opposition to that will, but to revenge himself for the insult now offered him," having also grown satisfied of his master's "entire unfitness for the throne."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke drives the point home with two devastating questions. "Why did he not do this before, when he knew that God had given the kingdom to David? Was he not now, according to his own concession, fighting against God?" And the reformed John Gill hears in the oath the swagger of a man usurping the divine prerogative, "insolently taking that to himself which belonged to the Lord, and as if he could not do it without him." The witnesses converge on a single, sobering irony: Abner will now serve God's promise — but from revenge, not obedience. His long resistance to the known will of God, broken at last not by repentance but by a wounded pride, becomes the instrument by which that will advances. The kingdom moves to David; the mover damns himself in the moving.
10:
Abner completes his oath by naming its object — "to translate the kingdom from the house of Saul, and to set up the throne of David over Israel and over Judah, from Dan even to Beersheba" — and the witnesses read the sweep of the phrase and the collapse of the king who hears it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the two verses together: Abner will transfer the whole realm to David, and Ishbosheth "could make no reply to these words of Abner, because he was afraid of him." The formula "from Dan even to Beersheba" (as in Judges 20:1) marks the totality of the transfer — not a province but the entire nation is in play.

The reformed John Gill hears again the arrogance he marked in the previous verse: Abner threatens "to translate the kingdom," which was precisely "what the Lord had sworn to do," yet he seizes the act as his own, "insolently taking that to himself which belonged to the Lord, and as if he could not do it without him." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends rather to what the vow reveals about the past: Abner's "sudden expression of resolve seems to imply that he had before had the matter under consideration," and thus that "there was some ground for the reproach of Ishbosheth" — the accusation over Rizpah touched a man already contemplating defection.

The verse thus does double work in the witnesses' reading. Forward, it announces the mechanism by which the long war of v.1 will finally end: the kingmaker of Saul's house has become the kingmaker of David's, and the promise of God, so long resisted, will be carried by the very hand that resisted it. Backward, it lights up the quarrel just past, suggesting that Ishbosheth's jealousy, foolish as it was, had fastened on a real disloyalty already forming in Abner's heart. All the commentators land on the same portrait of Ishbosheth — a king who cannot so much as answer his own general, silenced not by justice or argument but by naked fear. The house of Saul, which leaned wholly on Abner, now watches him openly plan its overthrow, and has not the spirit to say one word. The next verse will make that silence explicit; here the sentence of the dynasty is, in effect, already pronounced by its own captain.
11:
The narrative reduces the king of Israel to a single, eloquent sentence: "he could not answer Abner a word again." The witnesses read this silence as the death-rattle of Saul's dynasty, and they part slightly over whether it was cowardice or shrewd helplessness — but not over its cause. The reformed John Gill hears in it a man who dared not speak: Ishbosheth "would not, he durst not" answer, "otherwise, if it was fact he charged him with, he could have insisted on the truth of it, and aggravated the crime and scandal of it," reminding Abner that no past kindness could excuse it; "but such things, though he would, he durst not say." Fear, not lack of matter, sealed his lips.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the reason with a single stroke, translating the clause and its cause together: Ishbosheth "could make no reply to these words of Abner, because he was afraid of him." That naked dependence — a king afraid of the general who props his throne — is the whole tragedy of the house of Saul in one line.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke widens the scene into a maxim of statecraft. "Miserable is the lot of a king who is governed by the general of his army, who may strip him of his power and dignity whenever he pleases!" He reaches for later parallels — "the fate of poor Charles I. of England and Louis XVI. of France" — and lays down the rule his age had learned in blood: "Military men, above all others, should never be intrusted with any civil power, and should be great only in the field."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the same picture into a biblical image, recalling David's own words about his enemies: Ishbosheth, conscious of his weakness, said nothing "lest he should make bad worse," and became "like a bowing wall and a tottering fence" (Psalms 62:3). The witnesses do not really debate; they agree that this speechlessness is the verdict of providence. A throne that rests on a man's sword falls the instant that man withdraws it, and Ishbosheth's inability to answer is the moment the kingdom passes, in fact if not yet in form, from Saul's house to David's.
12:
Abner turns his threat into action and sends envoys to David, and the witnesses labour over the terse, twice-"saying" message — "Whose is the land?" and "Make thy league with me." The grammatical-historical commentators unfold both the wording and the two possible senses of the question. Keil and Delitzsch render Abner as sending "in his stead" (not, as some take it, "on the spot"), with the double message: "Whose is the land?" — that is, "to whom does it belong except to thee?" — and "Make a covenant with me; behold, so is my hand with thee, to turn all Israel to thee." Charles Ellicott lays the ambiguity bare: the words may mean "Is not the land thine by promise?" or "Who has the power to bring the land into subjection except myself?" Since the question grounds a proposed league, Ellicott judges the latter "evidently the sense intended," and "quite in accordance with the pride and haughtiness of Abner's character"; the repeated "saying," which has troubled commentators, he resolves neatly — the envoys were "charged first to represent the importance of Abner's influence, and then afterwards to say that he would exert it for David for a satisfactory consideration."

The reformed John Gill stresses that Abner sent "on his own account, and not on the account of Ishbosheth," secretly, "unknown to Ishbosheth"; the Targum reads that he sent from his own place, Mahanaim. The bargain is Abner's private treaty, struck for his own advantage.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley lifts the scene to its providential height. The envoys came "who in his name might treat with David"; and Wesley draws the marvel of it: "Thus God over-rules the passions of wicked men, to accomplish his own wise and holy purposes. And who then dare contend with that God who makes even his enemies to do his work, and destroy themselves?" On the question itself he takes the pious reading — "To whom doth this whole land belong, but to thee? Is it not thine by Divine right?" — the very truth Abner exploits for his own ends. So the witnesses hold two things together without strain: the message is Abner's cynical self-interest, dressed as homage; and it is, at the same time, God bending a proud man's ambition to serve the promise. Whether "Whose is the land?" flatters David's title or boasts Abner's power, the sentence carries the kingdom one long step nearer to its rightful king.
13:
David answers the overture with one condition, and the witnesses read his demand for Michal as a stroke at once of affection, of justice, and of shrewd policy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render David's reply — "Only one thing do I require of thee, namely, Thou shalt not see my face, unless thou first of all bringest me Michal, the daughter of Saul" — and read the motive as more than personal. Michal "had been unjustly taken away from him by Saul, after he had rightfully acquired her ... and given to another man" (1 Samuel 18:27; 1 Samuel 25:44), so that David "could demand her back again with perfect justice"; but Keil adds that the demand rested also "on political grounds." Charles Ellicott spells those grounds out: besides the justice of the claim and his affection for one "who had loved him and saved his life" (1 Samuel 19:11-17), the restoration "showed to all Israel that he bore no malice against the house of Saul," made him again Saul's son-in-law "and thus further his claims to the throne," and proved "that he was in a condition to enforce his rights as against the house of Saul."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke weighs the same balance of heart and statecraft. David already had six wives at Hebron, "but none of them could have such pretensions to legitimacy as Michal"; however painful the taking of her from a husband "who loved her most tenderly," prudence and policy required that David "not leave a princess in the possession of a man who might, in her right, have made pretensions to the throne." And she "was his own lawful wife, and he had a right to demand her when he pleased."

The reformed John Gill simply marks David's readiness to close the treaty — "he accepted of the offer ... ready and willing to enter into a covenant of friendship" — and to "forgive all past offences," pending this single stipulation. The witnesses are wholly agreed. David's one requirement is no romantic afterthought but a masterstroke, uniting fidelity to a wronged wife with a public act of reconciliation toward his rival's house and a quiet consolidation of his own right. In demanding Michal he claims his own, honours the fallen dynasty, and strengthens his title, all in a single condition — the mark of a man in whom, the commentators imply, natural feeling and providential wisdom moved together.
14:
David does not receive Michal from Abner's hand alone but sends a formal demand to Ishbosheth himself, and the witnesses agree that the detour through the king is a deliberate stroke of legality. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the motive precisely: David applied to Ishbosheth "that her return might take place in a duly legal form," so "that it might be apparent that he had dealt justly with Paltiel in the presence of his king, and that he had received his wife back again, and had not taken her by force from her husband." The demand rests on David's original bride-price — "whom I espoused to me for a hundred foreskins of the Philistines" (1 Samuel 18:25, 1 Samuel 18:27). Charles Ellicott makes the political point sharper still: the claim is pressed "upon the de facto king that all may be done legally," so that David "may not appear to be reclaiming his wife by force," while Ishbosheth "is thus compelled to acknowledge the wrong done to David and his inability to refuse his demand." Ellicott adds the exegetical nicety that David had actually paid two hundred foreskins, "but only one hundred had been required," and so only that number is named.

The reformed John Gill reconstructs the diplomacy behind the message: Abner could not himself deliver Michal, and so advised David to demand her of Ishbosheth "whose sister she was," promising then to use his own interest to secure the grant. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers the several strands into one: Ishbosheth's consent "was necessary, both to take her away from her present husband, and to persuade her to return to David"; the demand "opened to him a door of hope for his reconciliation, lest being desperate he should hinder Abner"; and David sought her "both for the affection he still retained to her, and upon a political consideration that she might strengthen his title to the kingdom." She is "my wife," Wesley insists — "who, though she was taken from me by force, and constrained to marry another, yet is my rightful wife."

There is no dispute among the witnesses, only a common admiration for the wisdom of the move. By routing his claim through the reigning king, David converts a private restoration into a public verdict: the house of Saul itself is made to confess, in the act of surrendering Michal, both David's legal right and its own inability to withhold it. Justice is done, and seen to be done, and David's title quietly grows in the doing.
15:
The verse is a single stark act — "Ishbosheth sent and took her from her husband, even from Phaltiel the son of Laish" — and the witnesses, few here, read it as the pathos and the powerlessness of the falling house made visible. The reformed John Gill notes only the plain fact and its history: Michal is taken "from her second husband, to whom Saul had given her" (1 Samuel 25:44), the marriage into which Saul had forced her when he tore her from David.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in the mechanics and the human weight of the transaction. Ishbosheth "probably sent Abner to Gallim to fetch Michal from her husband Paltiel, and take her back to David"; and the telling clause follows — "the husband was obliged to consent to this separation." The whole force of the sentence lies there: Phaltiel has no standing to refuse. What Saul's authority had joined, David's rising right and Ishbosheth's fear now put asunder, and the man who has kept Michal these years must simply let her go.

Behind the terse report the witnesses see the deeper current of the chapter. Michal is a woman moved twice by the will of kings — given away by Saul as an insult to David, reclaimed by David as an assertion of his own honour and right — and Phaltiel is caught in the wreckage of that royal contest. Keil's brief note that he "was obliged to consent" is itself a small tragedy, and it sets up the next verse's picture of him weeping along the road. Yet the commentators do not let sentiment obscure the justice of the case: Michal was David's lawful wife, taken from him by force, never rightly Phaltiel's at all. The verse thus holds two truths in tension that the witnesses keep steadily together — that David's claim is just, and that its execution costs another man real grief. Ishbosheth, who could not answer Abner a word, here cannot refuse David a wife; the same weakness that doomed his throne now dissolves a marriage at a word, and the daughter of Saul passes back toward the king her father had tried to keep her from.
16:
The camera lingers on Phaltiel weeping down the road behind Michal, and the witnesses divide gently — not over the facts, but over how much sympathy the scene deserves. The reformed John Gill reads his tears at face value: he went "along weeping behind her, because of his great affection to her, unwilling to part with her, but forced to it at the command of the king her brother." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the geography that measures his devotion: Abner halted him at Bahurim, on the road from Jerusalem toward the Jordan valley near the Mount of Olives, so that "Paltiel had therefore followed his wife to the border of the tribe of Judah, or of the kingdom of David" — a long way to walk in grief before the curt command, "Go, return," turned him back.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke feels the poignancy keenly and half-protests the whole proceeding: "If genuine affection did not still subsist between David and Michal, it was a pity to have taken her from Phaltiel, who had her to wife from the conjoint authority of her father and her king." Yet even Clarke bows to the law: "Nevertheless David had a legal right to her, as she had never been divorced, for she was taken from him by the hand of violence."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott holds the balance most exactly, and states the principle the others circle. Phaltiel "appears to have been sincerely attached to Michal, and it may be supposed that his affection was reciprocated" — the sympathy is real. "But," Ellicott adds, "it is to be remembered that she was not rightfully his wife, and that David's claim was prior as well as better." Here is the fine division of the witnesses: Gill and Clarke let the pathos stand almost unqualified, Clarke even calling it "a pity"; Ellicott insists the tears, however genuine, cannot overturn the right. None of them condemns David, and none of them mocks Phaltiel. They agree that the weeping husband is a true casualty of a just restoration — a man who loved a woman the law had never made truly his — and that his sorrow, checked at the border of Judah, marks the exact line where the claims of Saul's forced marriage end and the claims of David's lawful one resume.
17:
Abner now turns from his private treaty to public persuasion, opening negotiations with the elders of Israel, and the witnesses read his appeal as a mixture of true testimony and self-serving management. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the scene and the substance. Before setting out for Hebron, Abner "had spoken to the elders of Israel," reminding them, "Both yesterday and the day before yesterday" — that is, long since — "ye desired to have David as king over you. Now carry out your wish." Keil notes that Abner's word proves "even among the northern tribes the popular voice had long" leaned toward David; the general is not creating sentiment but releasing it.

The reformed John Gill describes the same activity in plainer terms: Abner "had a conference with the chiefs of the several tribes about the affairs of the kingdom," using his unrivalled influence to bend them toward David. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott supplies the historical warrant for Abner's claim, citing the long record of David's popularity — the women's song, his favour with all Israel, the tribes' later confession that even in Saul's day David led them out and in (1 Samuel 18:16; 1 Chronicles 11:1-3). It was, Ellicott observes, "the influence and activity of Abner that had hitherto prevented his general recognition as king."

The witnesses converge on a striking irony that they will develop through the following verses. Everything Abner now urges upon the elders is true — David was Israel's earlier choice, and the nation had long wanted him. Yet the mouth that speaks the truth is the very mouth that had for years suppressed it. The same Abner who "made himself strong for the house of Saul" and set Ishbosheth on the throne now tells the elders they had always wanted David, as though he had not been the one man standing in the way. Ellicott's remark exposes the whole: the obstacle to David's recognition is here recommending it. The commentators see in this no vindication of Abner's character but a fresh instance of the chapter's governing theme — that God makes even his opponents the messengers of his purpose, and that a truth long resisted loses none of its force when at last a self-interested man consents to speak it aloud.
18:
Abner clinches his appeal to the elders by invoking a divine word — "the LORD hath spoken of David" — and the witnesses divide over whether such a word stands recorded, while agreeing on the marvel of who is quoting it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the promise Abner cites: "By the hand of my servant David I will save my people Israel out of the power of the Philistines and all their enemies," and note that Abner presses this most upon Benjamin, Saul's own tribe, "because the existing royal family belonged to that tribe."

The difficulty is that no such saying survives verbatim, and here the witnesses debate. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke asks it bluntly: "Where is this spoken? Such a promise is not extant." He concludes it "means no more than, 'Thus, it may be presumed, God hath determined.'" The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott offers a fuller account of the options: the promise "may have been either an unrecorded utterance of one of the prophets (Samuel, Gad, or Nathan), or simply a reasonable inference from what had been promised, and from the Divine support of David in his career hitherto." Keil, for his part, is content that the substance of such a promise was widely known, even if its exact words are not preserved.

The reformed John Gill passes over the textual puzzle to the moral turn: Abner urges, "Now then do it," make David king, "and I shall no longer oppose it as I have done." That confession is the sting the witnesses feel most. Whatever the precise pedigree of the oracle, Abner treats God's purpose for David as settled and undeniable — and in the same breath admits that he himself has spent years opposing it. The debate over the missing text, real as it is, matters less to the commentators than the spectacle of Saul's own general preaching David's divine appointment to Saul's own tribe. The word of the Lord concerning David, whether spoken once by a prophet or gathered from his whole preserved history, has become so plain that even his fiercest former adversary can wield it as his decisive argument. The verse leaves the elders — and the reader — with the sense that resistance to David is now resistance to a declared purpose of God.
19:
Abner gives Benjamin a special conference of its own, and the witnesses agree that this careful attention to Saul's tribe is the shrewdest part of his campaign. The reformed John Gill explains why Benjamin needed separate handling: Abner "was of that tribe, and among them he had the greatest influence," and it was necessary "because of their attachment to the family of Saul, which was of that tribe," and because Benjamin lay so near Judah that its hostility could give David "a good deal of trouble" if unappeased.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the same logic. Charles Ellicott notes that "special and careful negotiations with the Benjamites were necessary, because they felt bound to their kinsmen of the house of Saul, and had hitherto enjoyed great advantages from their connection with their sovereign." Keil and Delitzsch add the grammatical observation that Abner's interviews with the elders and with Benjamin preceded his going to Hebron, and that "even among the northern tribes the popular voice had long" favoured David; Abner then went "to speak in the ears of David in Hebron all that seemed good to Israel, and that seemed good to the whole house of Benjamin." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley gathers the reasons for the special pains: to Benjamin Abner "particularly applies himself, because they might be thought most kind to Saul and his house, and most loath to let the kingdom go out of their own tribe," and because "they were a valiant tribe, and bordering upon Judah."

The witnesses read the verse, then, as a study in the political realism through which God's purpose advances. Benjamin had every reason to cling to Saul's line — kinship, honour, and the material advantages of holding the throne — and so required the most delicate persuasion. Abner, himself a Benjamite, was uniquely fitted to give it. Yet the commentators keep sight of the larger frame set in the previous verses: the man who once bound Benjamin to Ishbosheth is now, tribe by tribe, unbinding them and delivering them to David. The very influence that had propped the falling house is spent, at last, on completing its fall. There is no dispute among the witnesses here; they simply mark, with a kind of sober wonder, how thoroughly the human machinery of persuasion serves the divine word "the Lord hath spoken of David," and how the reluctant tribe is brought, by its own former champion, to the king it had most resisted.
20:
Abner comes to Hebron with twenty men and David makes them a feast, and the witnesses read both the retinue and the meal as weighty with meaning. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies the twenty: "doubtless representative men, selected by Abner from Israel and Benjamin to accompany him and confirm his report." And he insists the feast is no mere hospitality — it is "not to be understood of mere conviviality, but of a solemn sacrificial feast, such as was customary in ancient times in connection with important negotiations," citing the covenant-meals of the patriarchs and of Solomon (Genesis 26:30; Genesis 31:54; 1 Kings 3:15).

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the twenty as "representatives of all Israel, to confirm Abner's statements by their presence," and note the ominous phrase that closes the account of Abner's departure: David "sent him away, and he went in peace." Keil is careful with the wording — the text does "not state that David sent him away in peace (without avenging himself upon him), but that David sent him away, and he went in peace" — a distinction that "serves to prepare the way for what follows." The reformed John Gill marks the change in Abner's manner: earlier he had come to David "privately," but now, "having gained so many of the Israelites in favour of David, he appeared more publicly," bringing "some of the principal of the nation" to join in the league.

The witnesses thus set the scene at its hopeful height, with a dark thread deliberately drawn through it. Everything in the verse speaks of accomplishment and reconciliation: the delegation of twenty, the sacrificial feast that seals a covenant, the open and public league replacing the earlier secret overtures. The kingdom's reunion is all but done. Yet Keil's precise handling of "he went in peace" plants the warning the reader will need in a few verses' time — Abner leaves in peace, but the peace is his own condition, not a pledge of safety David has secured for him. The commentators, reading the whole chapter together, let the feast stand as the last untroubled moment before Joab's return. It is a covenant-meal in the fullest sense: a solemn, sacred confirmation that David is about to reign over all Israel — and the calm before the treachery that will nearly wreck it.
21:
Abner pledges to gather all Israel to David and departs in peace, and the witnesses, though few here, read the moment as the treaty's completion and the last calm before catastrophe. The reformed John Gill takes Abner's promise at its word: "I will arise and go" — "into the several parts of the land of Israel" — to gather the tribes and bring them under David's crown. The task Abner has begun with the elders and with Benjamin he now undertakes to finish nationwide, so that David may "reign over all that his heart desired."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the closing words — "he went in peace" — and reads them as a vindication of David's good faith: "David dismissed him in good faith, having no sinister design in reference to him." The point matters, for the murder that follows must not be laid at David's door; Abner left the king's presence safe, honoured, and trusted, with a covenant sealed and a commission accepted.

The grammatical-historical reading of the scene reinforces the point: Abner leaves Hebron not as a suppliant but as a plenipotentiary, entrusted with the reunion of the tribes, and David lets him go unguarded, a token of the good faith the murder will so cruelly betray.

Beneath the brevity of the verse the witnesses hear the chapter's deepest irony gathering. Abner speaks of David reigning "over all that thine heart desireth," and the commentators, reading with the wider chapter in view, catch the presumption in it: Abner measures David's heart by his own, crediting the future king with the very ambition that drives himself. Yet the words are also, unwittingly, true — God has indeed purposed to give David all Israel, and Abner is the instrument. The tragedy is that the instrument will never see the work finished. He goes out "in peace," having promised to complete the reunion of the kingdom, and within a few verses he will be dead at the gate of Hebron, struck down by the one man whose jealousy the peace could not reach. The witnesses let the verse stand as the high-water mark of Abner's usefulness and the threshold of his ruin: the covenant is made, the promise is given, the peace is real — and Joab is already on the road home.
22:
Joab returns from a raid to find Abner gone in peace, and the witnesses use the verse to set the stage for the murder, dwelling on the timing and on Joab's character. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agree on the nature of the expedition: Joab and David's servants came "from pursuing a troop" — a band of robbers, Philistines or the like, who had "taken advantage of a civil war between Israel and Judah" to raid the land, and whom Joab overtook and stripped of "a great spoil." Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, reads the same scene: Joab "returned elated with victory, and bringing great spoil; but Abner had concluded his interview and gone away before his return." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note the pointed repetition that "Abner was not with David in Hebron; for he had sent him away, and he was gone in peace" — the historian underscoring, before the blow falls, that Abner departed under David's safe conduct.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry opens here his long meditation on the whole tragedy, and begins with the collision of the two men. Joab, "upon his return," learned that Abner had just gone and that "a great many kind things had passed between David and him." Henry insists that Joab "had all the reason in the world to be satisfied of David's prudence and to acquiesce," knowing David "a wise and good man" under divine guidance; instead he presumed to chide his king. The verse, in Henry's reading, is the meeting of two currents that will end in blood: David's God-blessed statesmanship, sealing the reunion of Israel, and Joab's proud, jealous will, returning flushed with a soldier's success to find his rival honoured in his absence.

The witnesses do not divide; they align the facts so that the murder to come will be seen for what it is. Abner is gone in peace — the phrase is repeated so no one can mistake it. Joab arrives too late to prevent the league by argument and, as the next verses show, will undo it by treachery. The commentators mark the providential and moral irony: the very absence that let the covenant be sealed in peace is the absence Joab will exploit, fetching Abner back in David's name to a death David never willed.
23:
The report reaches Joab that Abner has come and been sent away in peace, and the witnesses read the verse as the fuse of the murder — the moment Joab learns what David has done and resolves to undo it. The reformed John Gill clarifies who brought the word and to whom: not the whole army but "the chief officers, who with Joab came to court, to wait upon David, and report their success," were told that Abner "the son of Ner came to the king, and he hath sent him away, and he is gone in peace."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the psychology that the bare report sets in motion. When Joab learned that Abner had been with David and "had sent him away again," he "went to David to reproach him for having done so," with the incredulous demand, "What hast thou done? Behold, Abner came to thee; why then hast thou sent him away, and he is gone quite away?" Keil sees Joab's design already forming: by painting Abner as a spy come "to learn thy going out and in ... and all that thou wilt do," Joab "hoped in this way to prejudice David against Abner, to make him suspected as a traitor," so that "he might then be able to gratify his own private revenge with perfect" cover.

The witnesses let the verse hang on the thrice-repeated refrain of the chapter — Abner "is gone in peace." It has now been said of David's sending, of Abner's going, and here of the servants' report; and each repetition sharpens the coming crime. Peace is the settled fact that Joab cannot bear. The commentators see in his reaction not honest alarm for the king's safety but the first move of a calculated treachery: the report of a peaceful, honourable parting is, to a jealous heart, intolerable news. Where David and Israel see reconciliation, Joab sees a rival advanced and a brother's death unavenged. The verse thus turns the good news of the covenant into the occasion of murder, and prepares the reader to distinguish sharply, as the following verses will, between the king who made peace and the captain who will shed "the blood of war in peace."
24:
Joab bursts in upon the king with an astonishing reproach — "What hast thou done?" — and the witnesses marvel at the insolence and probe the mixture of motives beneath it. The reformed John Gill pictures the entry: Joab, perhaps hearing the news at the palace door, "came to him in a passion," to the very apartment where the king sat. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the full arraignment — "What hast thou done? behold, Abner came unto thee; why is it that thou hast sent him away, and he is quite gone?" — and expose the slander folded into it: Joab charges that Abner "came to deceive thee, and to know thy going out and thy coming in, and to know all that thou doest," a portrait of Abner as a spy designed "to prejudice David against Abner."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott weighs the honesty of the outburst with care. Joab's "somewhat rough remonstrance" may have been "supported by an honest suspicion of Abner, for which there was some ground in Abner's long opposition to the known Divine will and his present revolt from Ishbosheth." Yet Ellicott sees the deeper springs: "there was also a personal enmity, due partly to the fear of being himself supplanted by an older and famous warrior, and partly to the desire to revenge the death of his brother Asahel," so that "Joab seeks to poison David's mind against Abner, that he may better carry out his revenge."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry is most scandalized by the sheer effrontery of subject to sovereign. Joab "chides David, and reproaches him to his face as impolitic," as though David "were accountable to him." Henry cannot decide which to wonder at more — "that Joab had impudence enough to give such an affront to his prince or that David had patience enough to take it." He notes that Joab "does, in effect, call David a fool," and that David returned no answer, "not because he feared him, as Ishbosheth did Abner, but because he despised him." The witnesses thus read the verse on two levels at once: as a study in the breathtaking arrogance of a general who presumes to school his king, and as the cunning first step of a premeditated murder, in which honest suspicion, wounded ambition, and blood-revenge are braided together and dressed up as concern for the crown.
25:
Joab presses his slander to its point — "Thou knowest Abner the son of Ner, that he came to deceive thee" — and the witnesses, though the verse is thinly attested, draw out the craft of the accusation and the truth it distorts. The reformed John Gill catches the rhetorical form of Joab's opening: it may be read as flat assertion, "Thou canst not be ignorant what a cunning deceitful man he is, nor of his designs," or, following the ancient versions, as a stinging question, "dost thou not know? art thou ignorant?" Either way, Joab appeals to Abner's known reputation as a shrewd and dangerous man to make his charge of espionage seem self-evident.

The verse rewards the reader who holds it beside the whole scene the commentators have been building. Joab's indictment has a plausible surface: Abner was a cunning man, he had long opposed David, and he had just abandoned the master he served — facts that lend color to the claim that he "came to deceive" and "to know all that thou doest." This is the cleverness of the lie: it is woven from half-truths. Yet the witnesses, reading with the chapter's earlier verses, know what Joab conceals. Abner came not to spy but to deliver the kingdom; he departed under a solemn covenant and David's safe conduct, "in peace." What Joab presents as prudent counsel for the king's protection is in fact the groundwork for a private revenge — the murder of the brother-slayer Asahel — and for the removal of a rival who might outrank him.

So the commentators let the single witness's grammatical note do real work. Whether Joab states or insinuates, he is manipulating David's knowledge of Abner's character against Abner's actual, changed conduct. The general who cannot bear that Abner should be trusted turns Abner's own history into a weapon, making past enmity the proof of present treachery. The verse stands as a case study in how a jealous man slanders: not by bald invention, which is easily refuted, but by pressing genuine facts about a man's reputation into the service of a false conclusion. David, who had just sealed peace with Abner in good faith, is being urged to see a spy where he had welcomed an ally — and the urging is itself the prelude to blood.
26:
Joab leaves the king and secretly recalls Abner, and the witnesses fasten on the two damning particulars: the recall was done in David's name but "David knew it not," and Abner returned in perfect, fatal trust. The reformed John Gill pictures Joab storming from the royal presence — going out "at once, as soon as ever he had spoken his mind, and flew out of the room in a great passion, not waiting for the king's answer" — which shows both Joab's fury and "how great his power was" over a king who dared not provoke him.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott handles the crucial question of whose authority summoned Abner back. Whether Joab acted "in his own or in David's name (though without his knowledge) does not appear," but either way, Ellicott observes, "Abner would readily suppose that the coming of Joab had made further conference desirable," and "his entire confidence in David is shown by his unsuspecting return." Ellicott adds the topography from Josephus: the well of Sirah lay some twenty stadia — "two and a half miles" — from Hebron, with a spring still called Ain Sareh north of the town, so that "Abner must have just left David when Joab arrived." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch agree that Joab sent the messengers "no doubt in David's name, though without his knowledge," and fetched Abner back "from the cistern of Sirah."

The witnesses read the verse as the machinery of treachery laid bare, and they are united on its moral. The clause "but David knew it not" is the hinge of the whole tragedy: it severs the king entirely from the crime and fixes the guilt on Joab alone. And Abner's willingness to turn back — supposing only that some further business with David remained — is precisely the measure of the peace David had made with him. He returns because he trusts the king; he dies because he trusts the captain. The commentators see here the special vileness of the deed taking shape: Joab does not challenge Abner in the field but lures him with the borrowed credit of the king's good faith, converting David's own reconciliation into the bait of an ambush. The nearness of Sirah only sharpens it — Abner is barely gone before he is called back to his death.
27:
Abner returns and Joab murders him in the gate, and the witnesses read every detail of the killing as an aggravation of its guilt. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch narrate it plainly: Joab "took him aside into the middle of the gate, to talk with him in the stillness, i.e., in private, and there thrust him through the body, so that he died for the blood of Asahel his brother" (2 Samuel 2:23). The pretext of a quiet word makes the treachery worse; the avenging of Asahel is named as Joab's plea.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke strips the plea of its cover. Joab "feared that, after having rendered such essential services to David, Abner would be made captain of the host: he therefore determined to prevent it by murdering the man, under pretense of avenging the death of his brother Asahel." The murder, Clarke says, was "one of the most unprovoked and wicked," and "such was the power and influence of this nefarious general, that the king dared not to bring him to justice for his crime" — as Joab would later prove by murdering Amasa the same way (2 Samuel 20:10). "Joab was a cool-blooded, finished murderer." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms both the setting and the mixed motive: the gateway was "a customary place of conference," where Joab awaited his victim; the stated reason was "the blood of Asahel his brother," but "no doubt his revenge was quickened by jealousy."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the deed on the twin scales of divine justice and human wickedness. On the one hand, "the Lord was righteous": Abner had "maliciously, and against the convictions of his conscience, opposed David," and had basely deserted Ishbosheth from pride and revenge, so that "God will not therefore use so bad a man ... in so good a work as the uniting of Israel." On the other hand, "Joab was unrighteous, and, in what he did, did wickedly." Henry exposes the hollowness of the blood-revenge: Abner had slain Asahel "in an open war," in self-defence, and only after fair warning refused; but Joab "shed the blood of war in peace" (1 Kings 2:5). He murdered treacherously, under pretence of "speaking peaceably" (Deuteronomy 27:24), his words "softer than oil, yet were they drawn swords" (Psalms 55:21), and did it in the gate — the place of judgment — "as one that neither feared God nor regarded men," and in Hebron, a Levites' city of refuge. The witnesses hold the two truths together without confusion: God's providence justly set Abner aside, and Joab's hand was justly cursed.
28:
Word of the murder reaches David, and his first act is to clear himself and his crown before God, and the witnesses read his solemn disavowal as both sincere protest and necessary policy. The reformed John Gill marks the timing that the text is careful to note: "it was some time after it was done that the news of it was brought to David," a circumstance recorded "the more to clear the king from any concern in this affair." David then declares, "I and my kingdom are guiltless before the LORD for ever from the blood of Abner."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the whole utterance and its imprecation: David pronounces himself and his kingdom "innocent before Jehovah for ever of the blood of Abner," and calls the guilt to "turn upon the head of Joab and all his father's house." Charles Ellicott underwrites the plea as simple fact: "This was true. Joab's act was entirely without David's knowledge, and was not only against his will on moral grounds, but was in danger of proving disastrous to him politically; hence he takes the strongest means of showing his abhorrence of the deed."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the disavowal as a model of a clear conscience under suspicion. Because Joab had gone so long unpunished, some might "suspect that Joab had some secret intimation from David"; therefore David "solemnly appeals to God concerning his innocency," and Henry draws the comfort of it: "It is a comfort to be able to say, when any bad thing is done, that we had no hand in it" (compare Deuteronomy 21:7). "However we may be censured or suspected, our hearts shall not reproach us." The witnesses do not divide; they agree that David's protestation is at once honest and prudent — honest, because he truly neither willed nor knew the crime; prudent, because a new and fragile throne could not afford the shadow of a treacherous murder falling across it. Yet the commentators also feel the unfinished quality of the moment, which the coming verses will press: David clears himself by word and curse, but the murderer still walks free, and a king's innocence declared is not yet a king's justice done.
29:
David turns his disavowal into a fivefold curse upon Joab's house, and the witnesses weigh both its terrible content and its questionable substitution of imprecation for justice. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch catalogue the curse: David prays that Joab's family may never lack "one that hath an issue, and a leper, and one who leans upon a stick, and who falls by the sword, and who is in want of bread" — the meaning being, "May God avenge the murder of Abner upon Joab and his family, by punishing them continually with terrible diseases, violent death, and poverty."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the severity as proof that Joab's deed could not be defended as a lawful avenger's act: Abner had killed Asahel "in battle, unwillingly and in self-defence," and Hebron was "a city of refuge" (Joshua 21:13), where "not even the Goel might slay the murderer without a trial" (Numbers 35:22-25). The curse falls on the whole "father's house" because Abishai too shared the crime. Ellicott also handles the disputed phrase "that leaneth on a staff," which some render "holding a distaff" — a man unfit for war — but he prefers the received sense. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke softens the tone by reading the verbs as prophecy rather than mere passion: "it will rest on the head of Joab," "a prophetic declaration, which sufficiently showed the displeasure of God against this execrable man."

Yet the witnesses do not let David off unexamined. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley asks the pointed question the whole scene raises: "would not a resolute punishment of the murderer himself have become David better, than this passionate imprecation on his posterity?" The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the same doubt: though "the guilt of blood brings a curse upon families," and "if men do not avenge it, God will," still "a resolute punishment of the murderer himself would better have become David than this passionate imprecation of God's judgments upon his posterity." Here is the real division among the witnesses — not over whether Joab deserved the curse, which none dispute, but over whether a king was right to curse when he ought to have judged. The commentators leave David's imprecation standing as both a just abhorrence of an atrocious crime and a troubling confession of a justice he felt too weak to execute.
30:
The historian names both brothers as the killers — "So Joab and Abishai his brother slew Abner" — and the witnesses attend to the shared guilt and to the excuse the verse both records and refutes. The reformed John Gill explains how Abishai comes to be charged though only Joab struck the blow: "Abishai was in the secret, and was consenting and advising to his death, and so an accessory to it," and, on Josephus's testimony, "was with him when he drew his sword, and smote him." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley states the principle exactly: "though Joab only committed the murder, yet Abishai was guilty of it, because it was done with his consent, and counsel, and approbation."

Both witnesses fasten on the reason the verse appends — that the brothers slew Abner "because he had slain their brother Asahel at Gibeon in the battle" — and both dismantle it as a justification. Wesley notes that Asahel's death was one "which he did for his own necessary defence; and therefore it was no justification of this treacherous murder." The verse itself, the commentators observe, undercuts the plea by the very way it words the two killings. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the distinction the Hebrew draws: the words for the two deaths "are different, the former denoting violence"; Abner is said to have merely "put to death" Asahel, but Joab and Abishai are said to have "slain" — murdered — Abner. "By this strong disapproval of Joab's act, David shows that it was done without his knowledge or consent."

Ellicott then names the flaw that will shadow David to his deathbed: the king "still remains at fault ... for continuing Joab in his high and responsible position," an inability to punish "so powerful a subject" which David "on his death-bed sought to remedy by his charge to Solomon" (v.39; 1 Kings 2:5). The witnesses thus read the verse as a careful legal verdict rendered by the narrator himself: two men, not one, are guilty; the blood-revenge is no defence, for it was war and self-defence that took Asahel; and the deed was murder, done without the king — even as it leaves the king exposed for failing to bring the murderers to account.
31:
David commands a public mourning and makes Joab himself lead it, and the witnesses read the funeral as at once genuine grief, political vindication, and a kind of penance imposed on the murderer. The reformed John Gill stresses how the command exposes the limits of David's power: he "enjoined his whole court, and Joab also, to express public mourning," yet Joab "did not flee, nor was he laid hold on in order to be brought to justice; which shows how great his power was." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke notes the pointed assignment — David "commanded him to take on him the part of a principal mourner" — turning the killer into the chief lamenter.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the whole ceremony as deliberate and layered: David "commands a public mourning with the usual signs of rent clothes and sackcloth, and lays this command especially upon Joab, who is thus required, as it were, to do public penance for his act," while "David himself followed the bier as chief mourner." The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley unfolds the several purposes. Joab was especially obliged to mourn "to bring him to repentance for his sin, and to expose him to public shame." And David's own following of the bier, "though this was against the usage of kings, and might seem below David's dignity," was nonetheless "now expedient to vindicate himself from all suspicion of concurrence in this action."

The witnesses hold these strands together without strain. The mourning is real — David grieves a man he had hoped to use and honour. It is also, unmistakably, a public act of state, designed to sever the crown from the crime in the sight of all Israel. And it is a rough justice on Joab, who cannot be executed but can be made to walk in sackcloth behind the man he murdered, doing penance in the eyes of the nation. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's larger meditation on the chapter frames the scene as David's determined effort to "wash his hands from the guilt of Abner's blood." The commentators see no contradiction between sincerity and strategy here: a godly king may weep truly and act wisely at once, honouring the dead, clearing his throne, and shaming the guilty, all in a single solemn procession to the grave.
32:
Abner is buried at Hebron and the king weeps aloud at the grave, and the witnesses read both the place and the tears as testimonies designed to be seen and believed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the choice of burial-place. Abner's family home, and so his natural resting-place, was at Gibeon (1 Chronicles 8:29), "but this may have been now under Ishbosheth's control," and in any case "a burial in the royal city of Hebron was more honourable and a more marked testimony to the grief of David." Where a man is buried says something; David buries his former enemy in his own capital, with honour.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record the scene at the graveside — "David wept aloud at his grave, and all the people with him" — and read it, with the funeral ode to follow, as calculated to "divest the minds of his opponents of all suspicion that Joab had committed the murder with his cognizance." The tears are real, but they are also evidence: the king's open weeping, joined by all the people, is a public disavowal more eloquent than any protest of innocence. The reformed John Gill adds the local color, noting the Jewish tradition that Abner "was buried in the midst of the city," contrary to the usual practice of burying outside, and wondering whether the nearby cave of Machpelah was then in use.

The witnesses read the verse, brief as it is, as the emotional center of David's whole response. Everything converges at the grave: the honourable place, the loud lament, the weeping nation. Keil's observation ties the moment to the chapter's central concern — that David be cleared, in the eyes of Israel, of any share in the treachery. Yet the commentators do not reduce the grief to strategy. David had seen in Abner "a prince and a great man," as the coming verses will say, and had hoped to make him a servant of the united kingdom; the tears at Hebron are the tears of a statesman robbed of an ally and of a man of feeling appalled by a base murder. The verse marks the point where private sorrow and public vindication become, for once, perfectly the same act.
33:
David sings a funeral lament over Abner — "Died Abner as a fool dieth?" — and the witnesses expound both the meaning of "fool" and the double edge of the question. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch gloss the key word: "The fool (nabal) is the ungodly, according to Israelitish ideas" (Psalms 14:1), and the sense of the ode is that Abner "had died a death which he did not deserve" — not the fettered, condemned death of a criminal, but a treacherous murder. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees: "as a fool" means "as a wicked man," and the question protests, "Was he cut off by the hand of justice for his crimes? Nothing less; but by Joab's malice and treachery."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke admires the poem's compressed force — "This lamentation, though short, is very pathetic. It is a high strain of poetry" — and reads it as "a heavy reproof to Joab ... being sung by all the people": Abner "was not taken away by the hand of justice, nor in battle, nor by accident: he died the death of a culprit by falling into the hands of a villain." The reformed John Gill simply frames it as the elegy David "had composed on this occasion," delivered now that his first tears were spent.

Beneath the agreement the witnesses hear a poignant complexity that the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, in his fuller comment on the passage, draws out: David speaks partly "as one vexed that Abner was fooled out of his life," that so shrewd and valiant a man should be caught off guard "and so die as a fool dies" — proof that "the wisest and stoutest of men have no fence against treachery" — and partly "as one boasting that Abner did not fool himself out of his life," dying not as a felon justly executed but as an innocent man falling before wicked men. Either way the lament is aimed at Joab. The commentators find in it David's finest stroke of vindication and grief combined: a king who cannot execute the murderer indicts him instead in a song the whole nation learns, turning Abner's undeserved death into a public monument to Joab's treachery.
34:
The lament's second strophe pictures Abner's freedom in the moment he was struck — "Thy hands were not bound, nor thy feet put into fetters" — and the witnesses read it as the sharpest indictment of the treachery. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase the meaning: "Thou hadst not made thyself guilty of any crime, so as to have to die like a malefactor, in chains and bonds; but thou hast been treacherously murdered." The effect on the crowd was to renew their grief — "this dirge made such an impression upon all the people (present), that they wept still more for the dead."

The reformed John Gill draws out the pathos of the unbound hands: Abner was no condemned criminal in fetters; and had he only known his danger, "as his hands and feet were at liberty, he might have defended himself; or ... made use of his feet and fled." His very freedom, unsuspecting, is what made him vulnerable. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sums the whole picture in "a few pregnant words": "Abner, so valiant in war, with his hands free for defence, with his feet unfettered, unsuspicious of evil, fell by the treacherous act of a wicked man."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley hears in the lines a note of grim honour for the dead and contempt for the killer. "Thou didst not tamely yield up thyself to Joab, to be bound hand and foot at his pleasure. Joab did not overcome thee in an equal combat, nor durst he attempt thee in that way, as a general or soldier of any worth would have done." Abner fell not by valor but "by the hands of froward, or perverse, or crooked men, by hypocrisy and perfidiousness, whereby the vilest coward may kill the most valiant person." The witnesses converge on one verdict, which the whole chapter has been building toward: the manner of the death is itself the proof of the crime. A man in fetters dies by law; a free man ambushed dies by treachery. Abner's unbound hands accuse Joab more loudly than any charge, and the people's fresh weeping is the nation's assent to the king's judgment that this was murder, not justice.
35:
The people press David to eat while it is yet day, but he swears to fast until sundown, and the witnesses read his refusal as the crowning proof of an unfeigned grief. The reformed John Gill supplies the custom the scene assumes: after a burial it was usual "to provide and send in food to the relations of the deceased, and come and eat with them," a practice, he notes, shared by Greeks and Romans, at which "kings themselves used to attend"; but "in this case David refused to eat with them," binding himself by oath to taste nothing "till the sun be down."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch weigh the exact sense of the people's approach, judging that they came not so much to hold the funeral meal with David as to urge him to "take some food, for the purpose of soothing his own sorrow" — and that David "mourned so bitterly" that he refused with a solemn oath. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the whole episode for its public effect: David's fasting "had already attracted attention, so that the people came to urge him to take food; but he utterly refused 'till the sun be down,' the usual time of ending a fast," and "David's conduct had a good effect upon the people."

The witnesses agree that this stubborn abstinence is the seal on all David's earlier acts of mourning. Words of innocence, a public funeral, tears at the grave, and a lament sung by the nation might, by a suspicious mind, be discounted as ceremony; but a king who will not so much as break bread until nightfall, and who binds his refusal with an oath, gives a proof that cannot be feigned. The commentators note the incongruity that David's example rebukes — the temptation, as Henry elsewhere puts it, "to turn the house of mourning into a house of feasting." Here the fasting king refuses that turn. The verse thus completes the chapter's careful demonstration: David neither willed nor countenanced the murder of Abner, and his sorrow is genuine to the point of self-denial. It is this transparent grief, the next verses will say, that finally satisfies all Israel of the king's clean hands.
36:
The people mark David's conduct and are pleased, and the witnesses read this popular approval as the political fruit of the king's sincere grief — and as a window into the health of his early reign. The reformed John Gill specifies what the people noticed: not merely the oath to fast, but David's "whole conduct at the funeral of Abner; the sorrow he expressed for his death, and the oration he made on account of it, in which he pretty severely reflected on his murderer." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the verse and its telling generalization: "all the people perceived it (i.e., his trouble), and it pleased them, as everything that the king did pleased all the people."

The wesleyan-arminian voices dwell on the substance behind the pleasure. Adam Clarke puts it plainly: "They saw that the king's grief was sincere, and that he had no part nor device in the murder of Abner." John Wesley adds that the people "were satisfied concerning David's integrity." The approval, in the witnesses' reading, is no mere courtly flattery but a considered verdict: the nation watched a king grieve, fast, and denounce the killer, and concluded that his hands were clean.
