The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, in his running comment, catches the mutual quality of the moment — "Whatever the king did pleased all the people" reveals both "his good affection to them," for "he studied to please them in every thing and carefully avoided what might be disobliging," and "their good opinion of him," so that "they thought every thing he did well done." Henry draws the domestic and social lesson: "Such a mutual willingness to please, and easiness to be pleased, will make every relation comfortable." The witnesses thus read the verse on two levels. Immediately, it records that David's transparent sorrow succeeded in its aim, clearing him in the eyes of all Israel at a moment when a suspicion of complicity might have shattered his fragile claim to the northern tribes. More broadly, it shows the bond of confidence between a godly ruler and his people, in which sincerity earns trust and trust interprets every act in the best light — the very opposite of the suspicion that had poisoned the house of Saul.
37:
The verse states the outcome David's whole day of mourning had aimed at — "all the people and all Israel understood that day that it was not of the king to slay Abner" — and the witnesses read it as the moment suspicion is finally dispelled. The reformed John Gill stresses the breadth of the acquittal: "not the people of Judah only, but of Israel also," came to know and were "satisfied by his conduct and behaviour, by his words and actions" that David had no hand in the crime.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse against the danger it removes. All Israel "discerned on that day — from David's deep and heartfelt trouble — that the death of Abner had not happened from the king, as many may probably at first have supposed, since Joab had no doubt fetched Abner back in David's name." There lay the peril: the murder had been committed under the borrowed authority of the king, so that the natural first suspicion would fall on David himself. It is precisely the visible sincerity of his grief that overturns that suspicion. Keil adds, looking to the next verse, that David's response also showed "how thoroughly David could recognise the virtues possessed by his opponents," hoping to have found in Abner "an able general and a faithful servant" whose murderer he "would at once have punished ... if he had only possessed the power."

The witnesses treat this verse as the hinge on which David's early reign turned. Coming to the throne of all Israel required the trust of the northern tribes, and that trust hung by a thread the moment their most influential leader was cut down in David's own city after leaving David's presence. Had the nation believed David complicit, the reunion Abner had brokered might have died with him. Instead, the commentators observe, the king's grief spoke louder than the circumstances: the very transparency of his sorrow persuaded a watching nation that the deed was Joab's alone. The verse records the quiet triumph of sincerity over appearance — a throne preserved not by force or argument but by a grief so evidently real that all Israel read the king's heart and absolved him.
38:
David turns to his own servants with a generous verdict on the dead — "Know ye not that there is a prince and a great man fallen this day in Israel?" — and the witnesses read the tribute as a mark of the king's magnanimity and discernment. The reformed John Gill notes the setting: these words were spoken not to the crowd at the grave but "to his servants at court," his courtiers, "giving a reason why he mourned as he did."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch draw out what the sentence reveals of David's mind. It "shows how thoroughly David could recognise the virtues possessed by his opponents, and how very far he was from looking upon Abner as a traitor, because of his falling away from Ishbosheth and coming over to him." On the contrary, David "hoped to find in him an able general and a faithful servant," and "would at once have punished the murderer of such a man, if he had only possessed the power" — the qualification that the next verse will make explicit.

The witnesses, joined here by the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry's fuller reflection, dwell on the justice of the praise. Abner's "alliance to Saul, his place as general, his interest, and the great services he had formerly done, were enough to denominate him a prince and a great man." Henry marks what David does and does not say: "When he could not call him a saint or a good man, he said nothing of that, but what was true he gave him the praise of." David honours the soldier and statesman without pretending to canonize the man's character — a measured tribute that praises real merit while passing over what could not honestly be praised. The commentators find in this the largeness of a heart that could weep for a former enemy and value an opponent's gifts. The lament over "a prince and a great man" is thus both a genuine estimate of Abner's worth and a further public repudiation of the murder: the king openly grieves the loss of a man he had meant to raise, not to destroy, and in doing so exposes once more the wickedness of the hands that struck him down.
39:
The chapter ends on the king's candid confession of weakness — "I am this day weak, though anointed king; and these men the sons of Zeruiah be too hard for me" — and the witnesses divide over whether to pity David's plight or to fault his failure of justice. The reformed John Gill reads the weakness politically, not personally: David was weak "not in body or mind, but with respect to the kingdom," which was "like a tender branch, or in its infant state"; anointed but not yet possessed of the whole realm, with Saul's son still holding the greater part, he could not yet wield the sword of justice against so powerful a subject. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke hears David's grievance that Joab and his brethren "have usurped all the power, and reduced me to the shadow of royalty," and notes that David "appears afraid to name" Joab, leaving him to God: "The Lord shall reward the doer of evil according to his wickedness."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the closing words as both explanation and confession. David's high estimate of Abner "shows that Joab's jealousy was not without ground," and "there is a tone of deep sadness in his words." Knowing the sons of Zeruiah for their "ungoverned passions, their bold lawlessness ... their great power and popularity with the army, he dared not punish them," and "leaves their judgment to God."

Yet the witnesses will not let the plea of weakness settle into an excuse. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley grants that David's danger "might give some colour to the delay of their punishment," but insists "it was a fault that he did not do it within some reasonable time," springing "from a distrust of God's power and faithfulness" and running "contrary to God's law, which severely requires the punishment of willful murderers." Wesley names it "carnal wicked policy, yea cruel pity that spared him," and observes with the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry that had the law taken its course against Joab, "the murder of Ishbosheth, Amnon, and others" might have been prevented (compare Genesis 9:6). Henry frames the enduring tension: David "ought to have done his duty, and trusted God with the issue." The chapter thus closes on a genuine division the witnesses keep open — David's inability was real, and yet his indulgence was a fault — while both sides look beyond it to the Son of David, whose kingdom, Henry adds, is "of a nobler kind," and who never fails, though he long delay, to punish impenitent wrong.
